request.question: Which was founded earlier, The Week or New Scientist?

title: Massachusetts Audubon Society
Pirate Party (Italy)
Jordan River Foundation
Champ de Mars Racecourse
Canons Regular of the Holy Sepulchre
The Week
Nominative determinism
Gloucester Old Bank
New Scientist
1883 Philadelphia Quakers season
sentences: The Massachusetts Audubon Society, founded in 1896 by Harriet Hemenway and headquartered in Lincoln, Massachusetts, is a nonprofit organization dedicated to "protecting the nature of Massachusetts."
Mass Audubon is independent of the National Audubon Society, and in fact was founded earlier.
Mass Audubon protects 36,500 acres of land throughout Massachusetts, saving birds and other wildlife, and making nature accessible to all with its wildlife sanctuaries and 20 nature centers.
The Italian Pirate Party (Italian: "Partito Pirata Italiano" ) is a political party in Italy, founded on 16 September 2006, modelled on the Pirate Party of Sweden, founded earlier that year.
It supports reform of copyright and patent law, privacy and freedom of expression.

The "Jordan River Foundation" was founded earlier by Queen Nour Al Hussein, the wife of the late king of Jordan Hussein bin Talal, after the death of king Hussein, queen Nour left her position as the Chair person of "Jordan River Foundation" and the new queen Rania took over the position.
The Champ de Mars Racecourse is a thoroughbred horse race track in Port Louis, Mauritius.

The Racecourse was inaugurated on 25 June 1812, by The Mauritius Turf Club (MTC) which was founded earlier in the same year by Sir Robert Townsend Farquhar, who was the first British Governor of Mauritius.
The Mauritius Turf Club is the oldest horse-racing club in the Southern Hemisphere and the second oldest in the world.
The race track follows a very selective right hand oval path and is relatively small in size, with a circumference of 1,298 meters (4,258.5 ft) and width between 12 and 14 meters (39 and 46 feet).
The home-straight extends uphill and is 225 meters (738 ft) long.

The Canons Regular of the Holy Sepulchre were a Catholic religious order of canons regular of the Rule of Saint Augustine said to have been founded in the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem, then the capital of the Kingdom of Jerusalem, recognised in 1113 by Papal bull of Pope Paschal II.
Other accounts has it that they were founded earlier, during the rule of Godfrey of Bouillon (1099–1100).
The Week is a weekly British news magazine founded in 1995 which has also published a US edition since 2001 and a children's edition in the UK since 2015.
Between 2008 and 2012 it also published an Australian edition.
Nominative determinism is the hypothesis that people tend to gravitate towards areas of work that fit their names.
The term was first used in the magazine "New Scientist" in 1994, after the magazine's humorous Feedback column noted several studies carried out by researchers with remarkably fitting surnames.
These included a book on polar explorations by Daniel Snowman and an article on urology by researchers named Splatt and Weedon.
These and other examples led to light-hearted speculation that some sort of psychological effect was at work.
Since the term appeared, nominative determinism has been an irregularly recurring topic in "New Scientist", as readers continue to submit examples.
Nominative determinism differs from the related concept aptronym, and its synonyms aptonym, namephreak, and Perfect Fit Last Name, in that it focusses on causality.
"Aptronym" merely means the name is fitting, without saying anything about why it has come to fit.
The Gloucester Old Bank was a British bank that operated between 1716 and 1838.
It was founded in 1716 by James Wood.
The bank was said to have been the oldest private bank in Britain, having survived the financial consequences of the Napoleonic Wars when many other banks went out of business.
The claim was wrong as both C. Hoare & Co and Child & Co. were founded earlier, it was, however, one of the oldest banks in Britain in the nineteenth century.
New Scientist is a weekly English-language international science magazine, founded in 1956.
Since 1996 it has also run a website.
The 1883 Philadelphia Quakers season was the first in the franchise's existence.
The team was founded earlier in the year as a replacement for the Worcester franchise.
It was the first year Philadelphia was represented in the National League since the original Athletics were disbanded in 1876.
The American Association's Philadelphia Athletics had been founded a year earlier.

candidate.summarize1.predict: Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: Which was founded earlier, The Week or New Scientist?
hop1_docs: New Scientist | New Scientist is a weekly English-language international science magazine, founded in 1956.  Since 1996 it has also run a website.

The New Times (magazine) | The New Times (Russian: Но́вые Времена́ ) is a Russian language magazine in Russia.  The magazine was founded in 1943.  The current version, established in 1988, , is a liberal, independent Russian weekly news magazine, publishing for Russia and Armenia.  (During the Soviet times it was a multi-language political magazine which followed the official party line.)  Its chief editor is Russian investigative journalist, political scientist, writer and radio host Yevgenia Albats.

Bat Boy (character) | Bat Boy is a creature who made several appearances in the American supermarket tabloid "Weekly World News".  "The Weekly World News" published patently fabricated stories which were purported to be factual.  Within the pages of the paper, Bat Boy is described as a creature who is 'half human and half bat'.  His pursuers, according to "Weekly World News", are scientists and United States government officials; he is frequently captured, then later makes a daring escape.  The original scientist who found him was named Dr. Ron Dillon.  Matthew Daemon, S.O.S. (Seeker of Obscure Supernaturals) crossed paths with him on several occasions.

Vinod Agarwal | Dr. Vinod K. Agarwal is an Indian-American businessman and scientist.  He was a professor at McGill University in Montreal, Canada from 1978 to 1992.  He then founded LogicVision, which he took public in October, 2001, as the first technology IPO following the terrorist attacks earlier that year.  Agarwal left LogicVision in 2005 to start SemIndia, a semiconductor company based in India.

Odisha Sampark Kranti Express | Odisha Sampark Kranti Express is a bi-weekly train which runs between Bhubaneswar,Capital of Odisha and Anand Vihar Terminal.  This train was introduced as part of the Sampark Kranti trains that were announced by the then railway minister Nitish Kumar in 2005.  It was and still is a bi-weekly train.  Now there is a requirement of attaching AC First class berths to this train.  Earlier the train used to terminate at New Delhi railway station (NDLS) but now terminates at Anand Vihar Terminus (ANVT) of New Delhi.  Its rakes are maintained by East Coast Railway (ECoR).

The Aquarian Weekly | The Aquarian Weekly is a regional alternative weekly newspaper based in New Jersey.  Founded in 1969, its focus is rock music and related events in the New Jersey/New York City/Eastern Pennsylvania region.  From 1986 to 1992, it was called East Coast Rocker.  After returning to its original title, the newspaper began including a pull-out section that retained the "East Coast Rocker" name, and which is now freely distributed throughout the region.

New Adventures | New Adventures is a British dance company, specialising in contemporary dance.  Founded by the choreographer Matthew Bourne in 2002, the company developed from an earlier company Adventures in Motion Pictures, which was later dissolved.
response.summary_1: New Scientist (founded 1956) was founded earlier than The Week (founded 1995).
response.hop2_query: New Scientist was founded earlier (1956) than The Week (1995).
hop2_docs: New Scientist | New Scientist is a weekly English-language international science magazine, founded in 1956.  Since 1996 it has also run a website.

55578 | 55578 (1995) is a compilation of earlier Wolfsheim works and new material.  It was released in February 1995 and remained on the German Media Control Charts for five weeks.  The album's name is the postal code for Wolfsheim, Germany.

Maxwell Heights Secondary School | Maxwell Heights Secondary School is a Canadian high school located in Oshawa, Ontario, within the Durham District School Board.  The school was established in September 2009 and is named after a previous school, Maxwell Heights Public School (1955–1995).  The school runs on the Modified School Calendar, starting a week earlier than schools on the traditional calendar, and closing for one week in November.

Rock music in New Zealand | Rock music in New Zealand, also known as Kiwi rock, began in 1955 when Wellington-based country singer Johnny Cooper (popularly known as the ‘Māori cowboy’) released a cover of American Bill Haley’s hit song ‘Rock around the clock’.  In 1956 Cooper wrote and recorded an original song, "Pie Cart Rock and Roll".  The song referred to a pie cart he visited while in Whanganui managing talent shows.  The song is believed to be New Zealand’s first indigenous rock ’n’ roll recording, though ‘Resuscitation rock’, written by Wellington teenager Sandy Tansley in March 1957, may have been released a few weeks earlier than Cooper’s song.

Early Career Life Scientist Award | The ASCB Early Career Life Scientist Award is awarded by the American Society for Cell Biology to an outstanding scientist who earned his doctorate no more than 12 years earlier and who has served as an independent investigator for no more than seven years.  The winner speaks at the ASCB Annual Meeting and receives a monetary prize.

New Zealand Association of Scientists | The New Zealand Association of Scientists is a professional body for scientists in New Zealand.  It was founded in 1942 as the New Zealand Association of Scientific Workers, and renamed in 1954.  It differs from the Royal Society of New Zealand in being an independent non-profit incorporated society and registered charity, rather than being constituted by an Act of Parliament.  While not being entirely non-political, the Association focuses on policy and social responsibility aspects of science.

This Week (ITV TV series) | This Week is a weekly current affairs series that was first produced for ITV in January 1956 by Associated-Rediffusion (later Thames Television), running until 1979, when it was replaced by "TV Eye".  In 1986, the earlier name was revived and "This Week" continued until Thames lost its franchise at the end of 1992.
response.summary_2: New Scientist (founded 1956) was founded earlier than The Week (founded 1995).
